case_title: DARRELL RINERSON VS. DAVID DYER ET AL

Off Calendar Jun-24-2019 Set For Order To Show Cause On Aug-20-19 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Plaintiff Darrell Rinerson'S Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Sustaining Demurrer To First Amended Without Leave To Amend
Off Calendar Jun-03-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Mark Carton, Esq. 415-567-5517 for Plaintiff.
Motaz Gerges, Esq. 818-396-4433 for Defendant Dyer through Court Call.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court shares it's views. Plaintiff Argues. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

Plaintiff Darrell Rinerson'S Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Sustaining Demurrer To First Amended Without Leave To Amend.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Plaintiff Darrell Rinerson's motion for reconsideration of the Court's April 10, 2019 order sustaining Defendant David Dyer's demurrer to the first amended complaint without leave to amend is denied. While Plaintiff seeks leave to file a second amended complaint based on certain facts that occurred after he filed his opposition to Defendant's demurrer, the new allegations he seeks to add cannot overcome the dispositive defects identified by the Court in its prior order. His attempt to reargue the Court's prior order is improper, and he fails to address controlling authority cited in that order. He also fails to show that the other arguments he asserts present new or different facts, circumstances or law that he could not with reasonable diligence have presented at the first hearing. (Code Civ. Proc. 1008(a); New York Times Co. v. Superior Court (2005) 135 Cal.App.4th 206, 212.)

Plaintiff asserts that on April 4, 2019, he "re-obtained complete ownership of 25% shares of ELI stock." (Mot. at 2.) However, Plaintiff admits that he sold his interest in ELI in April 2017, and he filed the original complaint in this action on August 9, 2018. As the Court indicated in its order, with exceptions not pertinent here, "California law 'generally requires a plaintiff in a shareholder's derivative suit to maintain continuous stock ownership throughout the pendency of the litigation." (Order at 2, quoting Grosset v. Wenaas (2008) 42 Cal.4th 1100, 1119.) Thus, Plaintiff's "now current ownership of ELI shares" (Mot. at 2) does not warrant reconsideration. "Plaintiff admittedly did not maintain continuous ownership in the company throughout the period this litigation has been pending, and therefore lacks standing to assert derivative claims on its behalf." (Order at 3.)

Defendant's Counsel, Mr. Gerges to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-20-2018 Continued To Feb-20-19 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Complaint
Off Calendar Dec-19-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Mark Carton, Esq., Telephone 415-567-5517, appearing for Plaintiff;
Arman Matevosyan, Esq., Shenon Law Group, Telephone 818-964-0310, appearing for Defendant David Dyer;

RULING - ARGUED: No appearance. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant David Dyer's anti-SLAPP motion to strike the entirety of the complaint filed by plaintiff Darrell Rinerson is granted as to the thirteenth and seventeenth causes of action for declaratory relief and conspiracy and denied as to all other causes of action. Mr. Dyer has not satisfied his first prong burden of showing that the claims arise from activity protected by the anti-SLAPP statute as to the first through eighth causes of action. Those claims arise from conduct allegedly committed by Mr. Dyer prior to his termination from ELI. While this lawsuit was proposed in emails in an effort to induce Mr. Dyer to settle his DLSE claims, the first through eighth causes of action are based on separate alleged wrongful behavior unrelated to Mr. Dyer's protected petitioning of the DLSE. "[A] claim may be struck only if the speech or petitioning activity itself is the wrong complained of'" (Park v. Board of Trustees of California State University (2017) 2 Cal.5th 1057, 1060.) The ninth through twelfth and fourteenth through sixteenth causes of action are not alleged against Mr. Dyer and thus he lacks standing to seek strike those claims. Mr. Dyer satisfied his first prong burden as to the thirteenth and seventeenth causes of action and Mr. Rinerson failed to satisfy his second prong burden of showing that those claims have minimal merit. The thirteenth cause of action for declaratory relief seeks to negate the effect the DLSE award and declare that debts are owed to Mr. Rinerson and ELI. This claim arises from Mr. Dyer's protected activity of petitioning DLSE for relief from allegedly owed compensation that was unpaid. (Levy v. City of Santa Monica (2004) 114 Cal.App.4th 1252 (claim seeking declaratory relief regarding administrative agency findings arises from anti-SLAPP statute protected activity).) The seventeenth cause of action for conspiracy also arises from Mr. Dyer's petitioning activity with DLSE. Mr. Rinerson did not present sufficient evidence to show that either the thirteenth or seventeenth cause of action has the required minimal merit to defeat an anti-SLAPP motion at the second prong stage. Because Mr. Dyer prevailed on only two of seventeen claims on this motion and did not achieved a significant reduction in the scope of the case yet his motion was not frivolous, both sides' request for attorneys' fees are denied. Order is signed.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: W. Trupek; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/HEK)
